critic_raw:
This is a well-constructed AML methodology with serious thought given to leakage, temporal splitting, and operational alignment. I'll identify the genuine structural issues rather than manufacture concerns.

**Issue 1: Label Incompleteness Creates a Systematic Bias That Temporal Buffering Does Not Solve**

The claim: analyst-confirmed cases, SAR filings, and AML-related closures constitute a reliable outcome label, and the 90-day maturation buffer ensures outcome observability.

Why it may be wrong: AML labels are not drawn from nature — they are drawn from the output of the prior detection system (rules-based or analyst-driven). Accounts that were never flagged by the existing system have zero probability of appearing in confirmed cases or SAR filings, regardless of their true suspiciousness. This means the label is not 'account is truly suspicious' but rather 'account was caught by the existing process.' This creates a classic case of outcome-dependent sampling: the negative class is not a clean negative, it is a mixture of true negatives and undetected positives. The 90-day buffer guarantees the outcome window is complete; it does not guarantee the outcome is observable for accounts that evaded detection. The consequence is that the model learns to replicate the existing detection system's coverage, not to extend it. If the GNN improves on the baseline by learning network motifs, some of that improvement may be real signal, but some is likely the model learning to replicate investigation pathways that are implicitly encoded in relationship data (shared investigators, referral patterns, co-flagging). The methodology acknowledges 'completeness of AML labels' is out of scope — which is honest — but does not acknowledge that this limitation actively biases the comparison between baseline and GNN, because network features may correlate more strongly with investigation artifacts than transaction features do.

Evidence: Compare the rate at which accounts with high graph centrality (many shared counterparties with investigated accounts) appear as labeled positives, controlling for transaction-level risk signals. If graph centrality predicts label status beyond transaction features, a portion of the GNN's advantage could be driven by proximity to existing investigative activity rather than intrinsic suspiciousness.

**Issue 2: The 90-Day Outcome Window Creates Label Leakage Through Investigation Artifacts in the Graph**

The claim: excluding case outcomes, SAR text, closure reasons, and post-investigation artifacts from features is sufficient to prevent leakage.

Why it may be wrong: Graph edges are built from transactions and relationships. When an account is under investigation (even before a confirmed outcome), investigators often request additional transaction records, subpoena counterparties, or freeze related accounts. These actions may generate transaction records, account status changes, or relationship flags that appear in the raw data and could propagate into the graph through edges between investigated and non-investigated accounts. More concretely: an account that will receive a confirmed SAR in month T+1 may, in month T, already have a co-transacting counterparty whose account has been frozen or flagged — and that status change, if it creates any edge attribute or removes edges from the graph (account closures removing nodes), implicitly encodes forward-looking investigative information. The methodology excludes 'post-investigation artifacts' but does not specify how investigation-in-progress signals are handled, which is the harder case.

Evidence: Check whether accounts with graph neighbors that were subsequently confirmed suspicious show elevated label rates beyond what transaction features would predict, using only accounts where the neighbor's confirmation date is after the scoring cutoff. If yes, the graph is carrying forward-looking signal through contemporaneous investigation side-effects.

**Issue 3: Precision@K Is the Right Operational Metric But the Wrong Selection Criterion for Comparing Models With Different Score Distributions**

The claim: mean monthly precision at top-K is an appropriate hyperparameter selection and comparison metric.

Why it may be wrong: The GNN and gradient-boosted tree produce scores from fundamentally different output spaces. The GNN's score distribution may be more sharply peaked or have heavier tails than the GBDT's, meaning top-K selection interacts with calibration in non-obvious ways. More importantly, precision@K is not monotone with respect to the underlying ranking quality when K is fixed and class prevalence varies month to month. In months where the base rate of suspicious accounts rises (e.g., due to a fraud wave or investigator capacity surge), precision@K can increase for both models simultaneously without either model improving in discriminative ability. Using mean monthly precision@K as the selection criterion will favor models that fit the high-prevalence months most aggressively, and if high-prevalence months are clustered in time (which they often are in AML), this can interact badly with the temporal split. The validation set is 6 months; if one model benefits from a particularly active investigative period in those months, hyperparameter selection will favor it in a way that doesn't reflect stable operational performance.

Evidence: Report precision@K by month and check whether month-to-month variance in precision@K is large relative to the train/validation/test difference. If the monthly standard deviation of precision@K within the test set exceeds the model comparison gap, the comparison is not stable. Also report whether confirmed case rates vary significantly across the 36-month window, as this would confirm base-rate confounding.

**Issue 4: The Ablation Design Cannot Isolate Network Structure From Merchant Risk Concentration**

The claim: removing merchant metadata from the graph isolates the value of network structure itself.

Why it may be wrong: 'Network structure' in this design is not separable from merchant metadata in the way the ablation implies. Merchant-neighborhood risk concentration is explicitly listed as a learned signal. When merchant metadata is removed, the edges connecting accounts to merchants still exist, but merchants become indistinguishable nodes. This does not remove the network structure — it degrades the node feature quality. The ablation tests 'network structure with uninformative merchant nodes' versus 'network structure with informative merchant nodes,' not 'network structure' versus 'no network structure.' To isolate network structure, the correct ablation is to replace the GNN with a model that uses the same node features but ignores edges entirely (i.e., a node-level MLP on GNN input features). The proposed ablation will understate the value of network structure if merchant nodes serve primarily as connectors between account nodes (in which case removing their features degrades the motif-detection capacity) and overstate it if merchant risk concentration is doing most of the work.

Evidence: Run the correct ablation — GNN with full features versus node-level MLP with identical input features but no message passing — and compare to the merchant-metadata-removal ablation. If the two ablations give different conclusions about the source of gain, the original ablation design was misleading.

**Summary:**
Issues 1 and 4 are the most structurally significant. Issue 1 is a fundamental label quality problem that biases the entire comparison in a direction that cannot be corrected post-hoc without access to ground truth outside the existing detection pipeline. Issue 4 is a design flaw in the key ablation that would yield an incorrect attribution of model gains. Issues 2 and 3 are real but addressable with the diagnostic checks described. The methodology is strong in its temporal handling, operational metric alignment, and leakage controls — the problems are not in those areas.
all_issues_raised: AML labels are outputs of the prior detection system, not ground truth, so both models learn to replicate existing investigative coverage rather than detect unobserved suspicious activity, and the GNN may benefit disproportionately because graph features correlate with investigation artifacts.

Graph edges built from pre-cutoff transactions may still carry forward-looking investigative signal if in-progress investigations cause contemporaneous account status changes or edge removals that are visible at scoring time.

Precision@K varies with monthly base rates independent of model discriminative quality, so hyperparameter selection and model comparison on mean monthly precision@K can be confounded by temporal clustering of high-prevalence investigative periods.

Removing merchant metadata from the GNN tests feature quality rather than network structure, because the structural ablation requires replacing message passing entirely with a node-level model, not degrading node features.
all_issues_adjudicated: AML labels are outputs of the prior detection system, not ground truth, so both models learn to replicate existing investigative coverage rather than detect unobserved suspicious activity, and the GNN may benefit disproportionately because graph features correlate with investigation artifacts.

Graph edges built from pre-cutoff transactions may still carry forward-looking investigative signal if in-progress investigations cause contemporaneous account status changes or edge removals that are visible at scoring time.

Precision@K varies with monthly base rates independent of model discriminative quality, so hyperparameter selection and model comparison on mean monthly precision@K can be confounded by temporal clustering of high-prevalence investigative periods.

Removing merchant metadata from the GNN tests feature quality rather than network structure, because the structural ablation requires replacing message passing entirely with a node-level model, not degrading node features.
